notes: Source 1 (newzealand.ai) returned 404 — substituted with AI Forum NZ Sep 2024 productivity report. Source 5 (AI Forum PDF) exceeded fetch size limit — substituted with AI Forum Aug 2025 report page. Creative industry sources (IDs 6-9) added 2026-04-26. RBNZ AN2026-02 added 2026-04-26.

sources.organisation: AI Forum of New Zealand, Te Herenga Waka Victoria University of Wellington, Callaghan Innovation
sources.contact: Madeline Newman, Executive Director, AI Forum NZ

key_stats.stat: 67% of NZ respondents are already using AI, particularly generative AI
key_stats.context: Inaugural biannual AI adoption snapshot across NZ businesses

key_stats.stat: 96% of AI-using businesses reported increased worker efficiency
key_stats.context: Among businesses already using AI

key_stats.stat: Over 50% of AI-using businesses experienced positive financial outcomes
key_stats.context: Financial costs described as remaining manageable

key_stats.stat: Minimal job displacement observed at time of publication
key_stats.context: New career opportunities emerging alongside AI adoption

sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ's inaugural systematic AI productivity snapshot showed high adoption (67%) with minimal displacement at the time — a baseline that has since shifted, with the 2025 follow-up showing job losses doubling to 14%.

sources.report_title: NZ AI Index 2025: State of AI Adoption and Barriers

key_stats.stat: 87% of large NZ organisations now use AI
key_stats.context: Up from 66% in 2024 and 48% in 2023 — near-doubling in two years

key_stats.stat: 89% of AI-using organisations report productivity gains
key_stats.context: Among large organisations currently using AI

key_stats.stat: 20% of organisations report productivity gains of 25% or more
key_stats.context: 35% report 10–25% gains; 28% under 10%

key_stats.stat: Only 14% of NZ workforces are confident and actively using AI
key_stats.context: 40% have moderate area-specific capability; 28% still building awareness

key_stats.stat: 50%+ of organisations experience 'shadow AI' — unapproved AI tool use by staff
key_stats.context: Employees adopting AI tools faster than employer governance allows

key_stats.stat: 50% of NZ business leaders believe NZ lags behind other countries on AI
key_stats.context: 49% not confident in government readiness for AI

key_stats.stat: 32% cite lack of internal capability and skills as the top AI barrier
key_stats.context: Second barrier: data quality/integration (22%); third: governance uncertainty (16%)

key_stats.stat: 46% of large NZ organisations still only exploring AI with pilots
key_stats.context: Only 12% have scaled AI deployment across their entire organisation

sector_detail.finance: Automation of checks and anomaly detection

sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ AI adoption among large organisations has nearly doubled since 2023, yet only 12% have achieved organisation-wide scale. 'Shadow AI' is widespread, with staff adopting tools faster than employers can govern — suggesting workforce exposure to AI disruption is outpacing organisational readiness.

sources.report_title: Generative AI Expected to More Than Double New Zealand's Productivity

key_stats.stat: $76 billion annually added to NZ GDP by 2038 — equivalent to 15% of GDP
key_stats.context: Optimistic scenario reaches $102 billion; downside risk of $33 billion loss if digital gaps persist

key_stats.stat: NZ workers are the third-fastest AI adopters globally
key_stats.context: Despite lagging enterprise-level adoption; described as a 'BYO AI' situation

key_stats.stat: NZ workers save an average of 275 hours per year (roughly 1 hour per working day) using AI
key_stats.context: Based on analysis of 19,000+ tasks across 400 occupations

key_stats.stat: 24% of NZ work tasks can be augmented by generative AI
key_stats.context: A further 14% of tasks could be fully automated

key_stats.stat: GDP gains could halve to $39 billion without strategic action on digital maturity
key_stats.context: NZ Treasury noted NZ is slower than peers in enterprise-level generative AI adoption

sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ has a paradox: workers are among the world's fastest individual AI adopters (3rd globally), yet NZ enterprises lag their international peers in systematic deployment. This creates a 'BYO AI' dynamic where workers are self-exposed to automation risk before their employers have governance in place.

sources.organisation: The Conversation / University of Auckland, Waipapa Taumata Rau
sources.report_title: AI is peeling back the layers of low-value work — NZ may be well-placed to adapt
sources.author: Kenny Ching, University of Auckland

key_stats.stat: 60–70% of activities in office support, customer service, and professional services can be automated
key_stats.context: McKinsey estimate cited; most affected: admin, legal, finance, compliance, marketing, consulting

key_stats.stat: AI could automate work equivalent to 300 million full-time jobs globally
key_stats.context: Goldman Sachs 2023 estimate

key_stats.stat: Approximately 70% of NZ exports come from agriculture, horticulture, seafood, and forestry
key_stats.context: Used to argue NZ's economic structure provides structural insulation from AI displacement

key_stats.stat: Most NZ job growth between 2000–2018 occurred in low-productivity service sectors
key_stats.context: These sectors are now among the most exposed to AI-driven restructuring

sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ's economy — anchored in primary industries requiring physical dexterity, sensory judgment, and human care — may be 'structurally insulated from the worst of the AI shock' compared to more service-heavy economies like the US or UK. However, the low-productivity service sector growth of the 2000s–2010s means a significant cohort of NZ workers hold roles now directly in AI's crosshairs.

sources.report_title: AI in Action: Key Findings from New Zealand's Third AI Productivity Report
sources.note: Original PDF URL exceeded fetch size; data extracted from published summary page

key_stats.stat: 14% of NZ businesses now attribute job losses directly to AI
key_stats.context: Doubled from 7% in the previous report — a 100% increase year-on-year

key_stats.stat: ~50% of AI-adopting NZ businesses report reduced need to hire new staff
key_stats.context: Hiring reduction is a leading indicator of structural job displacement

key_stats.stat: 91% of NZ businesses report efficiency improvements from AI
key_stats.context: Up from 96% in 2024; now measured across a broader sample

key_stats.stat: 77% of AI-using NZ organisations are reducing operational expenses
key_stats.context: 25%+ report annual benefits exceeding $50,000

key_stats.stat: 55% of NZ organisations have created new career opportunities through AI
key_stats.context: Growing demand for skills in data, governance, and digital transformation

key_stats.stat: 75% of NZ AI adopters report setup costs under $5,000
key_stats.context: Low barrier to entry accelerating adoption across business sizes

key_stats.stat: 44% of New Zealanders believe AI benefits outweigh risks
key_stats.context: Trust concerns are 'particularly acute among Māori and Pasifika communities'

sources.nz_specific_insight: Job losses attributed to AI in NZ doubled from 7% to 14% of businesses in a single reporting period — the sharpest single-year increase recorded. Simultaneously, 50% of adopters are reducing hiring, signalling structural workforce reshaping rather than isolated displacement. Trust gaps are deepest among Māori and Pasifika workers, raising equity concerns about who bears the burden of AI disruption.

sources.report_title: New Zealand Creative Industry Salary Survey 2026

key_stats.stat: AI has 'flattened' junior and BAU/content-focused creative roles in NZ
key_stats.context: Described as the defining hiring trend of 2025 — junior positions reduced or eliminated as AI handles routine output

key_stats.stat: Salaries experienced downward pressure in 2025; many candidates reducing expectations to secure roles
key_stats.context: Skilled candidate surplus created by restructuring and redundancies across NZ creative sector

key_stats.stat: Senior strategists with AI prompt expertise are increasingly valued and command premium rates
key_stats.context: Multi-skilled professionals who can work with AI tools commanding higher compensation

key_stats.stat: Developer positions (freelance and permanent) saw marked slowdown in 2025
key_stats.context: Both contract and permanent developer roles declined notably

key_stats.stat: Studios pivoting to lean permanent teams supplemented by specialist freelancers
key_stats.context: Structural shift away from permanent junior hires toward flexible, senior-weighted teams

key_stats.stat: In-demand roles: email/marketing automation specialists, paid ads (Google/Meta), social/video content creators
key_stats.context: Roles requiring platform expertise and video production skills are resilient

sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ creative industry data confirms AI is not yet causing mass job losses but has structurally eliminated entry-level and routine creative positions. The bifurcation is sharp: junior/BAU roles are being automated while senior AI-literate creatives are commanding higher salaries. This is the clearest NZ-specific validation that our O*NET scores underestimate risk for junior creative roles.

humanise_scoring_implications.junior_designers: +10 to +15 (explicitly flattened by AI in NZ)

sources.report_title: Music is at the forefront of AI disruption but NZ artists still have few protections

key_stats.stat: NZ's legal framework for AI and creative works described as 'woefully inadequate'
key_stats.context: No specific legal protection against AI deepfakes or AI training on NZ artists' work

key_stats.stat: NZ lags EU, Australia and Denmark in AI protections for creative workers
key_stats.context: Australia has begun legislating AI creative protections; NZ has no equivalent

key_stats.stat: Māori writer members of APRA AMCOS raised concerns about cultural appropriation and misuse by generative AI
key_stats.context: Māori creative works and te reo Māori content at specific risk of being scraped and reproduced without consent or cultural authority

roles_lower_risk: music technicians using AI for mixing/mastering (adaptive use)
sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ music creators face a regulatory vacuum — no consent requirements, no compensation framework, no deepfake protections. This amplifies automation risk beyond what O*NET scores capture, because NZ musicians face not just technological substitution but also legal non-protection for their existing work being used as training data. Māori artists face the additional dimension of cultural appropriation through AI scraping.
sources.maori_dimension: APRA AMCOS Māori members raised concerns about cultural appropriation; Te Mana Raraunga/Māori Data Sovereignty Network cited as a contrasting governance model for how cultural data should be handled.

sources.report_title: Musicians concerned by lack of regulation around fast-evolving AI technology

key_stats.stat: NZ operates as a regulatory 'wild west' with no protections or laws covering AI-generated music
key_stats.context: Current Copyright Act assigns AI-generated work to whoever prompted it, regardless of what training data was used

key_stats.stat: Government AI strategy report omitted creative industries implications entirely
key_stats.context: NZ government has not yet addressed how AI affects the creative workforce in its national AI strategy

key_stats.stat: AI music platforms (Suno, Udio) train on all scraped internet music without disclosing sources
key_stats.context: NZ musicians' recordings included in AI training data without consent or compensation

key_quotes.speaker: Sophie Burbery (musician/PhD student)
key_quotes.quote: Suno has admitted that all of its music is trained on anything it can scrape from the internet under fair use.

key_quotes.speaker: Clive Elliott KC (IP barrister)
key_quotes.quote: We have to find a way which compensates people.

key_quotes.speaker: Jeremy Toy (producer/artist)
key_quotes.quote: I find it offensive that people think they can train a computer to think independently like a creative.

roles_lower_risk: music technicians using AI for mixing/mastering
sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ musicians face compounded risk: technological substitution (AI can generate music in any genre) plus legal non-protection (NZ copyright law inadvertently rewards AI-generated output). The regulatory gap means NZ music creators have no recourse while their work trains the very systems replacing them.

sources.report_title: Aotearoa's Writers and Publishers Are Concerned About Works Being Used to Train AI

key_stats.stat: 71% of NZ writers and publishers are concerned about their work being used to train AI
key_stats.context: Survey of 120 NZ writers and publishers, Nov 2024 – Jan 2025

key_stats.stat: 50%+ of NZ writers and publishers are unsure whether their work has already been used for AI training
key_stats.context: Lack of transparency from AI companies means NZ creators don't know if they've been included in training datasets

key_stats.stat: 73% of those whose work was used for AI training did not consent
key_stats.context: Among respondents who are aware their work was used — non-consensual use is the norm, not the exception

sources.nz_specific_insight: NZ writers and publishers face a double exposure: their existing body of work is being incorporated into AI training datasets without consent or compensation, while AI simultaneously threatens to automate their future output. The 73% non-consent rate among those aware their work was used confirms this is not hypothetical — it is already happening to NZ creative workers.

sources.organisation: Reserve Bank of New Zealand (Te Pūtea Matua)
sources.report_title: Assessing AI and Robotics Exposure in the NZ Labour Market Using LLMs
sources.methodology: PRComp scoring — principal-component ensemble of scores from 10 LLMs (including Claude Opus 4.1, GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, LLaMA 3.1) assessing AI and robotics task exposure per ANZSCO occupation. AI and robotics scores combined into a joint PRComp index (0–1). Relevance to Humanise: methodology is directly comparable — Claude was one of the 10 models used.
sources.scale_note: PRComp score 0–1 where 1 = maximum exposure. To convert to Humanise 1–99 scale: multiply by 98 + 1.

key_stats.stat: 30% of NZ workers face high joint AI+robotics exposure (PRComp > 0.8)
key_stats.context: Joint exposure combines AI task substitution and physical robotics automation

key_stats.stat: 65% of NZ workers face moderate joint exposure (PRComp 0.2–0.8)
key_stats.context: The broad middle of the NZ workforce is in a significant transition zone

key_stats.stat: Less than 5% of NZ workers face low joint exposure (PRComp < 0.2)
key_stats.context: Very few NZ occupations are effectively shielded from AI+robotics disruption

key_stats.stat: Administrative workers face HIGHER AI exposure than managers
key_stats.context: Counterintuitive NZ-specific finding: admin roles more exposed than their managerial counterparts

key_stats.stat: Māori workers show significantly less AI exposure across most models
key_stats.context: Attributed to occupational concentration in sectors with lower AI exposure (primary industries, trades, community service)

key_stats.stat: Workers aged 30–39 and 50+ face highest AI exposure
key_stats.context: Mid-career and near-retirement workers more concentrated in automatable roles

key_stats.stat: Workers earning NZD $50k–$150k face the highest AI exposure band
key_stats.context: Mid-income bracket — not the lowest-paid physical workers, not the highest-paid executives

creative_roles_ai_only.note: High AI, zero robotics — a pure cognitive automation risk. Strong signal for Humanise.
High AI exposure. Confirms CLNZ survey findings. NZ creative writing roles at significant risk.

sources.nz_specific_insight: The RBNZ's LLM-ensemble methodology produces a fundamentally different risk picture than O*NET structural scores for two key occupation groups: (1) Software/tech workers score MUCH higher on RBNZ (PRComp ~1.0) than O*NET creativity/complexity scores suggest (~35–45 on Humanise scale) — reflecting AI's demonstrated ability to write code. (2) Physical workers (cleaners, sports, funeral) score MUCH LOWER on RBNZ (near zero) than O*NET routine scores suggest — reflecting that robotics deployment in NZ is limited. Humanise should use RBNZ as a NZ-calibration layer on top of the O*NET structural baseline.
sources.methodology_note_for_humanise: Because the RBNZ used Claude (Opus 4.1) as one of its 10 scoring LLMs, there is a direct methodological bridge between RBNZ findings and Humanise. This makes RBNZ the most citable NZ-specific validation source available.
